1: ὀφείλομεν (we are obligated, G3784) — the verb is of debt and moral necessity, not mere preference. Paul includes himself in the "we who are strong," establishing that the obligation to bear with the weak is a defining characteristic of the strong, not a concession they may choose to make. βαστάζειν (to carry, bear, G941) — the infinitive of purpose; the same verb used for carrying burdens in Galatians 6:2, where it describes mutual burden-bearing in the community.
2: ἕκαστος ἡμῶν τῷ πλησίον ἀρεσκέτω (let each of us please his neighbor) — present imperative: an ongoing obligation. εἰς οἰκοδομήν (for building up, G3619) — the construction metaphor that runs through Paul's ethics; pleasing the neighbor is not mere social compliance but serves the architectural goal of the community's flourishing. The phrase eis to agathon (for the good) qualifies areskō — pleasing must be directed toward actual good, not flattery.
3: καὶ γὰρ ὁ Χριστὸς οὐχ ἑαυτῷ ἤρεσεν (for Christ himself did not please himself) — aorist indicative: a completed historical act. The negative is emphatic; Christ's whole Incarnation and Passion is described as a sustained refusal of self-pleasing. τοῦ μὴ ἀρέσαι ἑαυτῷ — articular infinitive used as substantive subject of the quotation's application: the result of Christ's not-pleasing-himself is the weight of others' insults falling on him (Psalms 69:9 LXX).
4: ὅσα γὰρ προεγράφη (whatever was written beforehand, G4270) — aorist passive of prographō; the divine passive indicates God as the author of Scripture's purpose. εἰς τὴν ἡμετέραν διδασκαλίαν ἐγράφη (was written for our instruction) — another aorist passive; the purpose is explicitly pedagogical for the present community. διὰ τῆς ὑπομονῆς καὶ διὰ τῆς παρακλήσεως τῶν γραφῶν — through the patient endurance and the encouragement of the Scriptures; the genitives are subjective: Scripture itself produces these qualities in the reader.
5: ὁ θεὸς τῆς ὑπομονῆς καὶ τῆς παρακλήσεως (the God of endurance and encouragement) — Paul's characteristic way of naming God by the gifts he gives; the same divine attributes that the Scriptures produce (Romans 15:4) are sourced in God's own character. τὸ αὐτὸ φρονεῖν ἐν ἀλλήλοις κατὰ Χριστὸν Ἰησοῦν (to think the same thing toward one another according to Christ Jesus) — phroneo (to think/be minded, G5426) is the attitude/disposition verb Paul uses throughout Phil 2; the unity requested is not intellectual uniformity but the same orientation toward the other.
6: ὁμοθυμαδόν (with one accord, G3661) — a compound adverb built from homos (same) + thymos (passionate impulse, spirit); it appears frequently in Acts for the gathered community. The word describes a unified orientation of the will, not merely coordinated action. ἐν ἑνὶ στόματι (with one mouth) — the communal worship that results from the unified disposition; the glorification is verbal and liturgical, not merely attitudinal.
7: προσλαμβάνεσθε ἀλλήλους (receive/accept one another, G4355) — present middle imperative: the receiving is to be an active, ongoing practice. The same verb appears at 14:1 and 14:3 where God's receiving of both weak and strong is the ground for mutual acceptance. καθὼς καὶ ὁ Χριστὸς προσελάβετο ὑμᾶς (just as Christ received you) — aorist: a single completed act that defines the character of the community's ongoing receiving of each other.
8:
διάκονον γεγενῆσθαι περιτομῆς (to have become a servant/deacon of the circumcised, G1249) — the perfect infinitive indicates an ongoing resultant state; Christ's Incarnation placed him in the continuing role of servant to Israel. Diakonos here is the same word used for Phoebe in Romans 16:1 — a functionary serving a community's need. ὑπὲρ ἀληθείας θεοῦ (on behalf of God's truth) — genitive of the beneficiary; the mission to Israel serves the integrity of God's own covenant word.

εἰς τὸ βεβαιῶσαι τὰς ἐπαγγελίας τῶν πατέρων (to confirm the promises to the patriarchs, G950) — articular infinitive of purpose; bebaiōsai (to make firm, establish) is the verb for confirming a guarantee. Christ's mission to Israel was not a postponed mission to Gentiles but the necessary fulfillment of covenant promises that then opened the way for the Gentiles.
9: ὑπὲρ ἐλέους (on behalf of/for mercy, G1656) — here the Gentile glorification is grounded in God's mercy, contrasted with Israel's covenant-promise ground (Romans 15:8); both are reasons for the single mission. ἐξομολογήσομαί σοι ἐν ἔθνεσιν (I will praise you among the Gentiles) — future middle of exomologeō; the quotation from Psalms 18:49/2 Samuel 22:50 places the Davidic king among the nations in praise, which Paul reads as a messianic figure whose praise extends Israel's worship into Gentile territory.
10: The quotation from Deuteronomy 32:43 (the Song of Moses) — εὐφράνθητε, ἔθνη, μετὰ τοῦ λαοῦ αὐτοῦ (rejoice, Gentiles, with his people) — is a call for Gentile participation in Israel's covenantal joy. Moses's own closing poem already envisioned Gentiles rejoicing alongside Israel; Paul reads this as anticipation of the Gentile mission (cf. 10:19 where he quotes Deuteronomy 32:21 from the same poem).
11: Psalms 117:1 (LXX Romans 116:1) — αἰνεῖτε τὸν κύριον πάντα τὰ ἔθνη (praise the Lord all the Gentiles) — the shortest psalm and the broadest invitation; Paul uses it as a third OT witness to Gentile worship. The fourfold OT catena (Romans 15:9-12) from Psalms, Deuteronomy, Psalms, Isaiah demonstrates that the Gentile mission is not an afterthought but the convergence of multiple scriptural promises.
12: ἡ ῥίζα τοῦ Ἰεσσαί (the root of Jesse) — Paul quotes Isaiah 11:10 LXX; the Hebrew of Isaiah 11:10 has "root of Jesse" as a banner/signal, but the LXX (and Paul) read it as the one who rises up to rule. ὁ ἀνιστάμενος ἄρχειν ἐθνῶν (the one who rises to rule over the nations) — anistamenos is a present middle participle that can carry the double sense of rising geographically and rising from death; Paul may intend both the Messiah's reign and his resurrection as the ground of Gentile hope.
13: ὁ θεὸς τῆς ἐλπίδος (the God of hope, G1680) — the title summarizes the OT catena just completed: the God whose promises to patriarchs, Psalms, Deuteronomy, and Isaiah all pointed toward the hope now declared. πληρώσαι ὑμᾶς πάσης χαρᾶς καὶ εἰρήνης ἐν τῷ πιστεύειν (may he fill you with all joy and peace in believing) — aorist optative: the wish-prayer form; en tō pisteuein locates the filling within the ongoing act of trust. εἰς τὸ περισσεύειν ὑμᾶς ἐν τῇ ἐλπίδι (so that you overflow in hope) — perisseuein (G4052) is not merely having hope but brimming over with it; the goal of filling is abundance beyond sufficiency. ἐν δυνάμει πνεύματος ἁγίου (by power of the Holy Spirit) — the Spirit is the agent of the overflowing.
14: πεπληρωμένοι πάσης γνώσεως (filled with all knowledge, G1108) — the perfect passive participle indicates a standing state, not a process being initiated; Paul is not starting the Roman community's education but confirming what is already present. νουθετεῖν ἀλλήλους (to admonish/counsel one another, G3560) — noutheteō is a strong verb for corrective guidance; it implies both knowledge of the standard and will to apply it in the community context.
15: τολμηρότερον (quite boldly, comparative adverb, G5112) — the comparative form is an acknowledgment that some parts of the letter have pushed beyond courteous encouragement into frank challenge. ὡς ἐπαναμιμνῄσκων ὑμᾶς (as reminding you) — the participle frames Paul's letter as re-activating knowledge already possessed, not imposing foreign doctrine. διὰ τὴν χάριν τὴν δοθεῖσάν μοι (because of the grace given to me) — the aorist passive participle marks the apostolic commission as a gift received, not an authority self-claimed; cf. the same formula at Romans 12:3 and 1 Corinthians 3:10.
16:
λειτουργὸν Χριστοῦ Ἰησοῦ (liturgical servant/minister of Christ Jesus, G3011) — leitourgos is a civic and religious term for one who performs public service at their own expense; it carries cultic/priestly resonance. Paul frames his apostolic role in priestly vocabulary to ground what follows. ἱερουργοῦντα τὸ εὐαγγέλιον (performing priestly service with/for the gospel, G2418) — hierourgeō is a hapax in the NT; it means to handle something as a priest handles the sacred. The gospel is the sacred object of Paul's priestly service.

ἡ προσφορὰ τῶν ἐθνῶν (the offering of the Gentiles, G4376) — an ambiguous genitive: the Gentiles as the offering (objective), or the offering that the Gentiles bring (subjective). The context suggests the objective reading: Paul presents the converted Gentiles to God as a priestly offering. εὐπρόσδεκτος ἡγιασμένη ἐν πνεύματι ἁγίῳ (acceptable, sanctified by the Holy Spirit) — the Spirit performs the sanctification that makes the human offering acceptable, taking over the role the altar ritual performed for animal offerings.
17: τὴν καύχησιν ἐν Χριστῷ Ἰησοῦ (the boasting in Christ Jesus) — Paul's boasting is qualified by its location: not in his own achievements but in Christ. The dative en Christō specifies the sphere; his basis for glory is Christ's work, not his own. τὰ πρὸς τὸν θεόν (the things toward God) — the specific domain of his priestly role (cf. Hebrews 2:17; Romans 5:1).
18: οὐ γὰρ τολμήσω τι λαλεῖν ὧν οὐ κατειργάσατο Χριστὸς δι᾽ ἐμοῦ (I will not dare to speak of anything except what Christ accomplished through me) — the double negative creates emphatic restriction; Paul's preaching authority is entirely derivative. κατειργάσατο (aorist middle = accomplished, G2716) — Christ is the subject; Paul is the instrument. εἰς ὑπακοὴν ἐθνῶν (for the obedience of the Gentiles) — the same eis hypakoēn frame as Romans 1:5; the missionary goal is framed as covenant allegiance (obedience of faith).
19: ἐν δυνάμει σημείων καὶ τεράτων (in the power of signs and wonders, G4592/G5059) — the pairing sēmeia kai terata is the standard OT phrase for miraculous acts accompanying divine missions (Exodus 7:3; Deuteronomy 6:22); Paul claims this category for his apostolic mission. κεκληρωκέναι (perfect active infinitive of G2820, to complete/fulfill) — the perfect tense is significant: Paul's encirclement of the eastern Mediterranean with the gospel is presented as a completed mission in those regions, not an ongoing one. ἀπὸ Ἰερουσαλὴμ... μέχρι τοῦ Ἰλλυρικοῦ (from Jerusalem to Illyricum) — the geographical arc traces the actual mission; Illyricum is the northwest Balkan region, the farthest point Paul had reached.
20: φιλοτιμούμενον (G5389, making it my ambition) — a significant word; philotimeomai means to seek honor in a particular direction, to aspire deliberately; it is an honorable ambition rather than mere preference. εὐαγγελίζεσθαι οὐχ ὅπου ὠνομάσθη Χριστός (to proclaim the gospel where Christ has not been named) — aorist passive ōnomasthē (was named); the criterion for Paul's geographical planning is not institutional but pioneering: unplanted rather than unwatered. ἐπ᾽ ἀλλότριον θεμέλιον οἰκοδομῶ (I am building on another's foundation) — the construction metaphor Paul uses at 1 Corinthians 3:10 for the relationship between apostles and subsequent teachers.
21: Paul quotes Isaiah 52:15 LXX — ὄψονται, οἷς οὐκ ἀνηγγέλη (those who were not told will see) — as the scriptural warrant for his pioneer-mission strategy. Isaiah's Servant Song announces that kings will be silenced and see what they had not been told; Paul reads the Servant's mission as defining the scope of his own: going where the message has not yet gone. The scriptural grounding of his missional method is consistent with the letter's argument that the gospel's reach is scripturally anticipated.
22: τὰ πολλὰ ἐνεκοπτόμην (I was often/repeatedly hindered, G1465) — the imperfect passive indicates repeated action in the past; the hindering was not a single obstacle but a pattern. Paul does not specify the hindrance; it may be providential direction (cf. Acts 16:6-7), competing ministry demands, or adversarial opposition.
23: μηκέτι τόπον ἔχων ἐν τοῖς κλίμασιν τούτοις (no longer having place in these regions, G5117) — the "place" is the available space for pioneer proclamation; once the gospel is planted, Paul's work in that region is complete. ἐπιποθίαν δὲ ἔχων τοῦ ἐλθεῖν πρὸς ὑμᾶς ἀπὸ πολλῶν ἐτῶν (having a deep longing to come to you for many years, G1972) — epipothia is an intensified form of longing, beyond ordinary desire; the duration (many years) underlines the genuineness.
24: θεάσωμαι ὑμᾶς (behold/see you, G2300) — theaomai has a richer visual dimension than the plain horaō; it suggests a purposeful beholding rather than a passing sight. ὑμῶν προπεμφθῆναι ἐκεῖ (to be sent forward/assisted by you, G4311) — propempō is a technical term for the practical support (provisions, letters, travel companions) that a community provided for an itinerant missionary at departure.
25: διακονῶν τοῖς ἁγίοις (serving the saints, G1247) — present participle indicating the ongoing current activity that delays the Roman visit. The same diakoneo root appears throughout Romans 15 (Romans 15:8 Christ as diakonos, Romans 15:16 Paul as leitourgos, 16:1 Phoebe as diakonos); the term has both formal and informal service dimensions.
26: κοινωνίαν τινὰ ποιήσασθαι (to make a contribution/fellowship, G2842) — koinōnia carries both the spiritual dimension (participation, fellowship) and the practical (the financial sharing that expresses it). The same word Paul uses for the Lord's Supper in 1 Corinthians 10:16. εἰς τοὺς πτωχοὺς τῶν ἁγίων (for the poor among the saints) — the poverty is specific to Jerusalem; the collection is an act of Gentile solidarity with the mother community.
27: ὀφείλουσιν (they owe it, G3784) — the same obligation-verb as Romans 15:1 (we are obligated); obligation here is not legal but covenantal and relational. τοῖς πνευματικοῖς αὐτῶν ἐκοινώνησαν (they shared in their spiritual things, G2841) — the reciprocity Paul identifies is ontological and economic: Gentiles received spiritual inheritance through Jewish covenant; Jews now receive material support from Gentiles. The sharing (koinōnōnō) is the same root as koinōnia in Romans 15:26.
28: σφραγισάμενος αὐτοῖς τὸν καρπὸν τοῦτον (having sealed this fruit to them, G4972) — sphragizō is the verb for sealing as certification of delivery; Paul is treating the collection as a sealed consignment to be formally handed over. καρπόν (fruit, G2590) — the mission-harvest term (cf. Romans 1:13; Romans 15:20); the Jerusalem collection is itself described as fruit, implying it is the legitimate yield of the Gentile mission.
29: ἐν πληρώματι εὐλογίας Χριστοῦ (in the fullness of the blessing of Christ) — plēroma (G4138) is Paul's fullness-word; the blessing he will bring is not partial or provisional but complete. This confidence is grounded in Christ's provision, not Paul's preparation.
30: συναγωνίσασθαί μοι (to strive together with me, G4865) — the compound verb is from the arena; prayer is framed as athletic contest requiring real effort alongside the one praying. ἐν ταῖς προσευχαῖς ὑπὲρ ἐμοῦ πρὸς τὸν θεόν (in prayers on my behalf to God) — the double prepositions specify both direction (toward God) and beneficiary (on my behalf). διὰ τῆς ἀγάπης τοῦ πνεύματος (by/through the love of the Spirit, G4151 disp=3) — the genitive is probably subjective: the love that the Spirit generates in the community becomes the motivating ground of their prayer-striving.
31: ῥυσθῶ ἀπὸ τῶν ἀπειθούντων ἐν τῇ Ἰουδαίᾳ (that I may be delivered from the unbelieving/disobedient ones in Judea, G544) — apeithountes = those who disobey, refuse to be persuaded; the same root as Romans 11:30-32 where it describes Gentile and Jewish disobedience. Paul's prayer request anticipates the actual opposition he will face in Jerusalem (Acts 21:27-36). ἡ διακονία (the ministry/service, G1248) — the collection for Jerusalem is elevated to the status of a formal ministry.
32: ἵνα ἐν χαρᾷ ἐλθὼν πρὸς ὑμᾶς διὰ θελήματος θεοῦ (that coming to you with joy by God's will) — dia thelēmatos theou qualifies the arrival as conditional on divine permission; the joy is the anticipated texture of a visit completed under favorable conditions. συναναπαύσωμαι ὑμῖν (I may be refreshed/rested together with you, G4875) — the compound verb implies mutual refreshment; Paul anticipates the visit as mutually restorative, not merely ministerially productive (cf. Romans 1:12).
33: ὁ θεὸς τῆς εἰρήνης (the God of peace, G1515) — the last in the series of divine-character titles in this chapter (God of endurance Romans 15:5, God of hope Romans 15:13); this one is both a closing blessing and a forward pointer to 16:20 where the same title appears with eschatological content ("will soon crush Satan"). The peace Paul invokes is not merely relational but the comprehensive OT shalom — wholeness and right ordering.
